frequency_balance.description: Distribution of energy across the frequency spectrum, indicating tonal balance and potential mixing issues

sub_bass_20_60.why: Sub-bass provides weight and physical impact. Too much muddies the mix on small speakers; too little sounds thin.
sub_bass_20_60.how: Use high-pass filters on non-bass instruments (80-100 Hz). Use subtractive EQ on kick/bass. Check on subwoofer AND laptop speakers.
sub_bass_20_60.when_ignore: Acoustic music, jazz, classical rarely need heavy sub energy.

bass_60_250.why: Bass frequencies give warmth and power. This is where kick drums and bass guitars live.
bass_60_250.how: EQ bass and kick to occupy different spaces (use spectrum analyzer). Sidechain compression. High-pass guitars/vocals at 80-100 Hz.
bass_60_250.when_ignore: Lo-fi aesthetic, vintage recordings, acoustic folk.

low_mid_250_500.why: The mud zone - too much here makes mixes sound boxy and unclear. But it's also where body lives.
low_mid_250_500.how: Surgical cuts at 300-400 Hz on guitars/vocals. Use broad cuts (Q=0.7). Monitor on different speakers.
low_mid_250_500.when_ignore: Warm vintage tones, some genres intentionally embrace this warmth.

mid_500_2k.why: Where vocals and lead instruments live. Critical for clarity and definition.
mid_500_2k.how: Boost presence around 1-2 kHz for vocals. Cut competing instruments in this range. Use automation for emphasis.
mid_500_2k.when_ignore: Metal or rock with a scooped-mid sound is intentional in some productions.

high_mid_2_6k.why: Presence and clarity. Too much = harsh/fatiguing. Too little = dull/distant.
high_mid_2_6k.how: Use de-esser on vocals (3-8 kHz). Gentle boost at 2.5 kHz for clarity. High-shelf cut if too bright.

presence_6_12k.why: Air, sparkle, excitement. This is where cymbal sizzle and vocal breathiness live.
presence_6_12k.how: High-shelf boost +2-3 dB at 8-10 kHz for air. De-ess sibilance at 6-8 kHz. Use saturation for harmonic excitement.
presence_6_12k.when_ignore: Warm/dark productions. Excessive brightness causes fatigue.

air_12_20k.why: Extreme high frequencies add space and dimension. Many can't hear above 16 kHz.
air_12_20k.how: Subtle high-shelf boost. Use exciter plugins for harmonic generation. Don't overdo it.
air_12_20k.when_ignore: Most listeners over 30 cannot hear far above 15 kHz. It is not critical for most genres.

muddy_fix: Cut 250-500 Hz on guitars, pads, synths
High-pass filter everything except kick/bass at 80-100 Hz
Use spectrum analyzer to identify frequency conflicts
Solo bass and kick together - they should complement, not fight
harshness_fix: Check for distortion/clipping in this range
Reduce cymbal harshness with narrow cuts at 3-4 kHz
thin_fix: Boost bass at 80-120 Hz (but check on small speakers)
Add warmth at 200-300 Hz to key elements

balanced.note: Deep sub, controlled mids, clear highs

integrated_loudness.description: Perceived loudness over the entire track duration, measured in LUFS (Loudness Units relative to Full Scale)

educational_content.why: Integrated loudness measures perceived volume over the full mix. Streaming normalization means louder is not automatically better.
educational_content.how_to_fix_loud: Reduce limiter threshold, back off makeup gain, inspect bus compression, keep master fader at unity, and A/B against reference tracks.
educational_content.how_to_fix_quiet: Add measured makeup gain with a limiter, keep ceiling around -0.3 dBTP, confirm you are not leaving excessive headroom, and compare RMS with references.
educational_content.when_to_ignore: If you are printing for mastering, working on classical or jazz, or deliberately preserving wider dynamics, do not force a streaming loudness target too early.
educational_content.context: Streaming platforms generally normalize toward the low-to-mid teens LUFS, so crushed masters lose punch and are often turned down anyway.

dynamic_range.description: Difference between the loudest and average levels, indicating compression amount and dynamic variety

educational_content.why: Dynamic range shows how much contrast remains between peaks and the average level. Low DR means heavy compression and a flatter emotional arc.
educational_content.how_to_improve: Back off master limiting, prefer parallel compression, automate section energy, and leave choruses room to open up.
educational_content.when_to_ignore: EDM, some modern metal, radio singles, and hyperpop may intentionally run lower DR than acoustic or orchestral material.
educational_content.context: Streaming normalization changed the value of loud masters. Preserved dynamics now translate better than over-limited loudness.

stereo_width.description: How wide the mix feels across the stereo field and how safely it collapses to mono.

width_strategy: Keep low end mono (< 120 Hz): kick, bass, sub elements
Center: lead vocals, kick, snare, bass
Wide: pads, reverbs, background vocals, delays
Use correlation meter - keep above -1, ideally above +0.5
common_mistakes: Widening everything (creates phase mess and loss of punch)
Not checking in mono (surprises on club systems)
advanced_techniques: Mid-Side processing: boost Side highs, keep Mid lows
Use stereo imager on bus groups, not every track
Pan drum overheads vs. close mics for depth
Automate width: verse narrow, chorus wide
Use tools like Ozone Imager to visualize

balanced.note: Wide enough for excitement, safe for mono

reference_approach: Start with mono mix to get balance right
Add width gradually, checking mono compatibility
Final width should feel natural, not exaggerated
If it sounds too wide in headphones, it probably is
